result:
The world is made of mornings. Not the grand, sweeping epiphanies, but the quiet, unheralded ones. The first sip of tea that tastes like earth and rain, not just hot water. The precise geometry of a spiderweb in the corner of the porch, beaded with dew, a masterwork constructed in the dark. The way the cat stretches, a slow, deliberate uncurling of every vertebra, a lesson in how to inhabit a body completely.

We have become curators of the monumental, archivists of the declared important. We document the peaks and ignore the gentle, rising slope that got us there. We have forgotten the texture of things. The cold, smooth underside of a river stone. The dry, papery rustle of oak leaves in November, holding on long after the maples have gone bare. The satisfying *thock* of an axe splitting seasoned wood, the clean separation along the grain.

Sometimes, I think we are haunted by the ghosts of a million unattended moments. The conversation we rushed through to get to the point. The walk where we planned tomorrow’s meeting instead of feeling the crunch of gravel underfoot. The meal eaten staring at a screen, the flavors just a vague accompaniment to someone else’s curated life. These ghosts don’t wail; they simply accumulate, a silent, weightless mass of lost time, making the air feel thick with absence.

There is an art to noticing, and it is a dying one. It requires a kind of voluntary poverty—a shedding of the mind’s clutter to afford the richness of the immediate. To watch a bumblebee, that fuzzy, aerodynamic impossibility, bumble from clover to clover. To follow the path of a single cloud as it erodes in the wind. To really listen to the rain—not as a nuisance or a ambiance track, but as a symphony on a tin roof: the patter, the drumroll, the steady hiss.

I knew a man once who could identify trees by the sound the wind made in their branches. The pine’s sigh, the poplar’s chatter, the oak’s low, steady murmur. He lived in a small, cluttered cottage and seemed to possess nothing of value. And yet, he was the richest person I’ve ever met. His wealth was in his attention. He had paid the entry fee, in quiet hours and patient looking, to a world most of us hurry past.

We speak of “killing time” as if it were an enemy to be slain. But what if time is not an adversary, but a medium? Like clay or watercolor. We can’t kill it; we can only shape it, or let it slip through our fingers, formless. The tyranny of the clock has convinced us that only the productive moments count. That an hour spent watching shadows lengthen across a floor is an hour wasted. But what is it wasted *from*? The real transaction isn’t between you and the clock; it’s between you and your own aliveness.

Perhaps the deepest rebellion left to us is immovable attention. To stand in a checkout line and feel no itch for your phone, but to notice the weary kindness in the cashier’s eyes. To be stuck in traffic and watch the interplay of brake lights, a red river flowing backward. To look at a dandelion pushing through a crack in the asphalt and see not a weed, but a poem about relentless, golden insistence.

We are starved for wonder because we have neglected the humble diet of the ordinary. The extraordinary is not hidden on a mountaintop or in a distant galaxy; it is layered within the familiar, waiting for a shift in focus. The vein in a leaf is a map. The condensation on a glass is a temporary galaxy. Your own hand, with its network of lines and the pulse beating softly at the wrist, is a mystery you carry with you every day.

So here is my suggestion, for myself and for anyone listening: Miss your train. Not on purpose, but when it happens, don’t immediately rage and recalibrate. Look up. The station has its own life. The patterned grime on the windows, the echo of a thousand footsteps, the stranger’s face etched with a story you’ll never know. Let the world flood in. It is all data. It is all connection.

Because in the end, what will we have but the collection of our attentions? The love we paid real witness to. The peace we found not in escape, but in deep immersion in a single, fleeting moment. The cup of coffee truly tasted. The conversation truly heard. The evening sky, observed until the blue deepens to indigo and the first star trembles into being, a punctuation mark in the vast, quiet sentence of the night.

This is the quiet work of a life: not to build a legacy, but to have truly inhabited the days we were given. To have felt the sun and the rain on our skin, to have known the names of a few trees and birds, to have left a few moments so utterly unburdened by past or future that they became, in themselves, eternal. The world is made of mornings. And afternoons. And the deep, soft dark. It is all here, waiting for us to stop, and look, and begin.
